title: Reading and Use of English - Part 8
description: Read the article about four people who changed their careers. For questions 47 – 56, choose from the people (A – D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: A New Path: The Mid-Life Career Switch

texts.A:
### Elena, the Former Corporate Lawyer
'I had spent fifteen years climbing the ladder at a top-tier law firm,' Elena recalls. 'The prestige was great, but I was emotionally hollow.' [52]The decision to leave wasn't a sudden epiphany; it was a slow erosion of interest.[/52] [48]When I told my colleagues I was opening a boutique bakery, they reacted with a mix of pity and disbelief.[/48] They couldn't understand trading a six-figure salary for flour and yeast. [55]Initially, the transition was terrifying, especially the financial uncertainty.[/55] But now, when I see a customer smile at a sourdough loaf, I feel a sense of utility I never found in a courtroom. It’s a physical exhaustion, but a mental peace.
texts.B:
### Marcus, the Former Software Engineer
Marcus was at the top of his game in Silicon Valley when he decided to become a secondary school teacher. 'I was tired of creating apps that just encouraged more scrolling,' he says. [49]I wanted to see the immediate impact of my work on human beings, not just on metrics.[/49] [53]I did have to go back to university for a year to get my teaching qualification, which was a humbling experience at forty.[/53] [50]I found that many of the problem-solving skills I'd developed in coding were surprisingly applicable to managing a classroom of thirty teenagers.[/50] It’s a different kind of stress, but far more rewarding.
texts.C:
### Sarah, the Former Marketing Executive
Sarah left a high-pressure role in London to become a traditional furniture maker. 'I was constantly living in the future—planning campaigns for next year,' she explains. 'I needed to work in the present.' [51]The biggest challenge wasn't learning to use the tools, but learning to accept that things take time.[/51] In marketing, everything is instant; in carpentry, you can't rush the wood. [47]I was lucky to have a partner who supported me financially during the first two years of my apprenticeship.[/47] [56]Without that safety net, I’m not sure I would have had the courage to follow my hobby as a profession.[/56] It was a gamble that paid off in happiness, if not in wealth.
texts.D:
### David, the Former Investment Banker
David’s shift to organic farming was driven by a health scare. 'My body simply told me: no more,' he says. [54]The physical demands of farming were a shock to my system after twenty years at a desk.[/54] [52]Unlike some who plan for years, I handed in my resignation the day I left the hospital.[/52] [50]People think farming is simple, but the data analysis involved in soil health is as complex as any financial model I ever handled.[/50] [49]What I love most is the direct connection between my effort and the harvest.[/49] I’m not moving invisible money anymore; I’m growing something you can eat.

questions.question: mention receiving financial assistance from another person?
questions.explanation: Sarah mentions being lucky to have a partner who supported her financially during the first two years.

questions.question: describe a negative or skeptical reaction from peers?
questions.explanation: Elena says her colleagues reacted with a 'mix of pity and disbelief' when she mentioned the bakery.

questions.question: express a desire for a more tangible or visible result of their work?
questions.explanation: Marcus wanted to see 'immediate impact on human beings' and David (D) mentions the 'direct connection' and growing something 'you can eat'.

questions.question: mention that skills from their previous job were useful in their new one?
questions.explanation: Marcus found coding skills useful for classroom management. David (D) also compares soil analysis to financial models.

questions.question: state that the main difficulty was adjusting to a different pace of work?
questions.explanation: Sarah says the biggest challenge was learning that 'things take time' compared to the 'instant' nature of marketing.

questions.question: contrast a gradual decision-making process with a sudden one?
questions.explanation: Elena (A) says her decision was a 'slow erosion', while David (D) says he resigned the day he left the hospital, unlike those who plan for years.

questions.question: mention the need to obtain a new formal qualification?
questions.explanation: Marcus had to go back to university to get a 'teaching qualification'.

questions.question: admit to being unprepared for the physical toll of the new job?
questions.explanation: David says the physical demands of farming were a 'shock to my system'.

questions.question: mention feeling a sense of dread or fear during the initial stages?
questions.explanation: Elena states that the transition was 'terrifying' at the beginning.

questions.question: acknowledge that their career change might not have happened under different circumstances?
questions.explanation: Sarah says 'Without that safety net, I’m not sure I would have had the courage'.
